collaborators.interiorismo: Mariana de la Garza e Iván Zúñiga

sensory_thesis: We thought of the horizon as this thread — sometimes easily noticeable while other times not so much — that tells this story. A horizon that exists once every moment, that is never the same for others nor to oneself if you look at it twice. The one who is always in touch with time, that implies contemplation and gives you the gift of observation. The horizon, which is only beautiful if one gazes with beauty. The place where the sky touches the sea, one that sometimes fades away, and that keeps transforming forever.
sensory_thesis_source: Texto nuestro — written by the Oficio Taller team for this project
climate_context: Pacific coast of Baja California Sur. Semi-arid, ocean-fronting site. Strong wind, salt air, dry scrub landscape meeting the Pacific. Site documented in both rain and sun (January–February 2023).
emotional_intent: Contemplation. The need to look outward as far and wide as possible. The blue of distance — that deeper, dreamier, melancholy blue where land dissolves into sky. A house that gives you the horizon as a living condition, not a view.
site_observations: Site photos from January–February 2023. Documented in rain and in sun. The site is at the edge of sand and surf — Cerritos beach, BCS. The horizon line is the central organizing element of the project. Sky and sea are indistinguishable at distance.

literary_references.text: The places are what remain, are what you can possess, are what is immortal. They become the tangible landscape of memory, the places that made you, and in some way you too become them.
literary_references.source: Rebecca Solnit, A Field Guide to Getting Lost

literary_references.text: The blue at the horizon, the blue of land that seems to be dissolving into the sky, is a deeper, dreamier, melancholy blue, the blue at the farthest reaches of the places where you see for miles, the blue of distance.
literary_references.source: Rebecca Solnit, A Field Guide to Getting Lost

literary_references.text: It is here, on this edge of sand and surf, where I must have developed my need to see the horizon, to look outward as far and wide as possible.
literary_references.source: Terry Tempest Williams, When Women Were Birds

literary_references.text: Although it is always there, you can only see it if you really look for it. No two landscapes are exactly the same, but they are all linked by this line on which we rest the desire to continue looking.
Silence as musicality and as a constant whisper that speaks to us about deep water and deep blue, about the infinite horizon and the origin of all things.
literary_references.source: Teresa Moller and Jimena Martignon, Reflections in the Landscape

intake_notes: Sensory thesis and literary references extracted directly from the architects' own publication text document (HORIZONTES TEXTOS PUBLI, Google Doc, modified Aug 2024).
